projects.earlyyears.title: site.hopen
The Early Years!
site.hclose
site.br
projects.toobig.title: site.hopen
Too Big Matt! Too Big!
site.hclose
projects.toobig.discussion.1: My old Pa was out, working, in the other yard one day, though he was young back then. We had two yards separated by a 6' paling fence. A week or two, earlier, he had mowed the long grass and left it where it fell (we were semi-rural, and more rural than semi). My father was burning some rubbish, I think, in the other yard. I decided that I needed my own fire — out behind the dunny — but, as these things occasionally do when one overestimates one's abilities to control a situation, the fire had gotten away from me.
projects.toobig.discussion.2: My father came around in time to hear me saying, "Too big, Matt! Too big!". That might have been my way of trying to soften the magnitude of my faux pas. Although my brother was younger, he had taught me to light matches without burning myself. Ma soon learnt to hide matches but, alas, by the time I was twelve, I had a dozen different ways to start a fire:-D Now, in later life, I've learnt to control it — mostly — and to great effect.
projects.toobig.discussion.3: This was not the final assault on the old dunny but it was certainly the most spectacular, and the one that came closest to its wooden elements being reduced to ashes.
projects.thefuse.title: site.br
site.hopen
A bang and spark — and a blown fuse!
site.hclose
projects.thefuse.discussion.1: I was still in primary school — Year 6. I had asked Pa if he could purchase a Photovoltaic cell for me. He came home with a light-controlled switch module and mounting for it. It was not what I wanted for the project in question but I set about wiring it up using a 100W incandescent light as a load.
projects.thefuse.discussion.2: The latency of the opto-electromechanical switch provided a useful tool for demonstrating feedback loops. :-D I took my assembly out to Pa, when he arrived home from work, so he could see it. He looked at it — an odd blend of dubious horror and curiosity. He placed it on the floor, switched it on, and was immediately greeted by the same feedback that I had discovered a little earlier.
projects.thefuse.discussion.3: He was not long home from Work. Ma had been out to pick him up from the railway station. Whilst she was out, and before I had wired the light, I decided it would be a good idea to short the active and neutral to a 2 cent piece, no doubt, to see what would happen. I was greeted with a flash, ozone, the smell of burning insulation, and a blown fuse.
projects.thefuse.discussion.4: In panic, I thought, "Awww shit!!!". I ran out to the meter box to inspect the fuse that I expected that I had blown. When I pulled the ceramic block my suspicions were confirmed and impressively so — copper was splattered all over the casing! I found the fuse wire, selected the correct rating and set about repairing the damage. Ma and Pa never knew.
projects.shockingblue.title: site.br
site.hopen
A Shocking Education!
site.hclose
projects.shockingblue.discussion.1: Primary school was a busy time. I possessed something of a cruel streak, though I had never perceived it thus — I liked giving other kids electric shocks. I justified it by not doing unto them what I had not done to myself, a thousand times before. Of course, I did not take into account the element of surprise and betrayal that the poor unfortunates also had to accommodate. It just added to my mirth, I'm almost ashamed to say... The funny thing was that every kid I shocked followed along so that they could watch the next victim. Also surprising was the fact that I could convince one kid after another to hold the two wires. The shock was delivered by the ignition coil from a lawn mower with a couple of D-cell batteries in series.
projects.uniyears.title: site.hopen
University
site.hclose
site.br
projects.undergrad.title: site.hopen
Another Fire
site.hclose
projects.undergrad.discussion.1: I was in my third year, studying Microbiology and Immunology and attending a lab class. We were supposed to be culturing bacteria on agar plates. The two supervising academics were out front and one was yapping incessantly. Students did what student do and started fidgeting. We were supplied with wire loops, for inocculating plates; alcohol, for sterilizing the loop, and a bunsen. The process involved dipping the loop in alcohol, flaming it, dipping it in liquid culture, then inoculating the plate. The alcohol had been supplied in plastic beakers:-| (What could possibly go wrong?). Whilst the lecturer was yapping, one of the students set fire to the alcohol in the beaker, then, sat there with her hand raised whilst the lecturer droned on. Realization was heralded by a melting, deformed beaker! Panic! The lecturer swiped her lab coat past the beaker and spread the fire.
projects.undergrad.discussion.2: It was almost comical. The 2IC did the right thing and covered the minor conflagration with his lab coat (it was closer to hand than a fire blanket) and smothered the fire. Unbelievably, he leaned over the desk and WHOOMP! The hot fumes reignited and singed the 2IC's eyebrows off.
projects.undergrad.discussion.3: Whilst this little melodrama played out, someone at my desk did the same thing. We had a burning beaker. Being as acquainted with fire as I was, I grabbed a piece of paper and placed it between my hand and the beaker, effectively smothering the fire. My fellow student was, I think, grateful for being saved from the same indignity. About two minutes later, the beakers were replaced with glass beakers.
projects.honours.title: site.hopen
Honours — Another Fire
site.hclose
projects.honours.discussion.1: I'd opted to steer away from biology and focus more on chemistry. to that end, I'd pursued an advanced chemistry project that led into my honours year. I was running HPLC separations for a large part of that year. I was consuming litres of hexane/ethyl acetate — or at least, my experiments were. With he volume of solvent I was pumping through the HPLC system, I wanted a larger stock-bottle in which to store my solvent mix. I found one in the store room and it was the grottiest thing I'd seen in awhile. It had some sort of residue that I had been unable to remove with alcohol, acetone, or hot water + detergent. Not even aqua regia would move it.
projects.honours.discussion.2: Chromic acid was the last resort. I decided to make it in situ. My supervisor was working in the lab that day — a rare occurrence, and she had her back to me. I had already loaded two spoons of Chromium Trioxide into the filthy stock bottle and I was in the process of loading a third when I thought I heard a sizzling noise. I stopped, looked to the side of the bottle and I thought I saw a dull orange flash. I took a step back and was rewarded for my efforts with the all too familiar "WHOOMP!!", followed by a rain of green soot, and a fire.
projects.honours.discussion.3: My supervisor heard the noise and turned to see what had happened. In a mildly annoyed but quiet voice, she ordered, "Put that fire out!". I picked the bottle up to swing it to the bench behind me. It was then that she became a little agitated and squawked, "Don't pick it up!". I thought to myself, "If she only knew what I had on my bench, she'd understand!". I ignored her moved the bottle, then covered it with a piece of paper and my hand. She again squawked, "Don't put your hand over it!! Put the stopper in it!!". I thought, "No, there's probably an excess of oxidising agent. If there is any more unreacted reducing agent, the bottle will explode. If there's an excess of reducing agent, then covering the bottle with my hand is the safest option to avoid an explosion, or a sustained fire that risks shattering the bottle."
projects.honours.discussion.4: What my supervisor was unaware of was that I had about 40 open vials with  about 20-30mL, each, of EtOAc/Hex mixture. I had two large 4L winchester bottles, both open, and I also had an open bottle of diethyl ether. The latter has a flash-point of -40 Dec. C, and it heavier than air. It was a warm day - it could very well have become much, much warmer.
